parentQuestion: A patient develops a rare autoimmune condition where antibodies attack the tight junctions between endothelial cells lining blood vessels throughout the body. Which physiological consequence would you predict?

scaffolds.q: Blood vessels have walls that control what enters and leaves the bloodstream.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The vessel wall acts as a selective barrier.

scaffolds.q: TIGHT JUNCTIONS seal the gaps between cells, controlling permeability.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! They're like gaskets that prevent leakage between cells.

scaffolds.q: When tight junctions are damaged, fluid and proteins can LEAK out of vessels.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The vessel wall becomes 'leaky' and substances escape.

scaffolds.q: EDEMA is swelling caused by fluid accumulating in tissues.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Fluid that should stay in blood vessels leaks into surrounding tissue.

scaffolds.q: Damaged tight junctions → increased permeability → fluid leaks into tissues.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This causes widespread edema throughout the body.

scaffolds.q: This condition would cause WIDESPREAD EDEMA as vessels leak everywhere.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Every blood vessel in the body would become leaky.

scaffolds.q: Clotting and blood pressure are NOT directly affected by tight junction damage.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Those involve different mechanisms than tight junction integrity.

scaffolds.q: Attacking tight junctions causes WIDESPREAD EDEMA from leaky vessels.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Increased vascular permeability = fluid accumulation in tissues.

scaffolds.q: The answer is WIDESPREAD EDEMA - fluid leaking from vessels into tissues.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not oxygen delivery (hemoglobin issue), not clotting, not vasodilation.
